frame_analyses.description: At 15 seconds, both instruments already in field and actively engaged near drain. Left instrument (serrated jaws clearly visible) and right instrument working with needle and suture. Very fast entry.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Fastest instrument engagement yet. Needle already being positioned for driving by 15 seconds.

frame_analyses.description: At 37 seconds, both instruments working near drain. Suture and needle visible — needle appears to have been passed through the drain. Suture thread extends between instruments through the tissue.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement approaching completion by 37 seconds — this is the FASTEST of any attempt. Previous best was ~40s completion in post-2 and post-practice. Confident, smooth needle driving visible.

frame_analyses.description: At 52 seconds, suture wrapping visible. Loop of suture above the right instrument near the drain. Classic throw wrapping configuration. Both instruments engaged.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First throw beginning at ~48-52 seconds is the fastest to reach the throwing phase of any video. The wrapping appears controlled and deliberate.

frame_analyses.description: At 66 seconds, left instrument (serrated jaws visible) has suture looped. Active wrapping for surgeon's knot. Right instrument positioned near drain holding the free end.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Surgeon's knot wrapping in progress. The double-loop pattern is visible around the left instrument.

frame_analyses.description: At 81 seconds, instruments repositioned. Suture visible looped above left instrument in upper portion of frame. Second throw wrapping in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Quick transition from first to second throw. Instrument positions suggest hand change from the first throw.

frame_analyses.description: At 96 seconds, both instruments close to drain surface. Right instrument jaws open near the knot. Suture thread visible. Tightening or completing the third throw. Drain slit edges appear to be closing.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Third throw tightening. The slit closure is visibly improving — edges are coming together. All three throws completed by ~100 seconds.

frame_analyses.description: At 118 seconds, instruments working near the drain. Suture loop visible above the right instrument. Cutting phase appears to be in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting phase. Instruments positioned near the knot for suture tail cutting.

frame_analyses.description: At 133 seconds, left instrument near drain with suture loop. Right instrument appears withdrawn or positioned for final cut. Suture tail visible.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Completing the suture cutting. The cutting phase is taking about 25-30 seconds, slightly longer than post-2 (18s) but well within range.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 147 seconds. Instruments fully withdrawn. Completed knot clearly visible on the Penrose drain — dark suture thread forming a compact cross pattern. The drain slit shows GOOD CLOSURE — edges well-approximated by the knot. Short suture tails visible. Drain remains securely on suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: BEST RESULT OF THE ENTIRE SERIES. The fastest completion time (147s vs previous best 158s) combined with good slit closure, compact knot, and short suture tails. This represents a 26% time improvement from the pre-training baseline and the first time all quality markers are met with a sub-150 second time.

knot_assessments.notes: Frames 8-10 show double-loop wrapping around left instrument consistent with surgeon's knot. Wrapping is confident and controlled. Confidence moderate.
Frame 12 shows repositioned instruments for second throw. Instrument positions suggest hand switch occurred. Confidence low-moderate.
Frame 14 shows third throw tightening. Final result confirms excellent slit closure and secure knot. Confidence moderate-high based on final result quality.

technique_summary: This seventh and final video represents the BEST performance in the entire training series — a new personal best by every metric. Completion time of approximately 142 seconds is 16 seconds faster than the previous best (post-2 at 158s) and 56 seconds faster than the pre-training baseline (198s), a 28% improvement. Every phase was faster: needle loading at 10 seconds (best yet), suture placement at 30 seconds (best yet, down from 50s at baseline), and individual throws averaging 22 seconds. The final result shows excellent slit closure with a compact, secure-appearing knot and appropriately short suture tails. The drain was not avulsed. This video demonstrates that the regression seen in post-3 through postV6 was likely fatigue-related, and with fresh performance the trainee has not only recovered but exceeded their previous peak. The trainee has clearly developed improved laparoscopic skills.
improvement_suggestions: Suture cutting still takes ~30 seconds — with overall time now at 142s, cutting is 21% of total time. Target 15 seconds to push below 130s total
Continue maintaining the efficient 10-second needle loading — this is now approaching expert-level speed
Suture placement at 30 seconds is excellent improvement but expert level is 20 seconds — continue refining needle driving angle
The throw phases at 20-24 seconds each are close to efficient — target 15-18 seconds for further gains
strengths: NEW PERSONAL BEST — 142 seconds, 28% faster than baseline, 10% faster than previous best
Every single phase improved from any previous video
Needle loading at 10 seconds — approaching expert efficiency
Suture placement at 30 seconds — major breakthrough from the 40-62 second range in recent attempts
Excellent slit closure — compact knot with short tails, no gap
All three throws completed correctly in sequence
Drain not avulsed — consistent good tissue handling maintained throughout training
Confident, purposeful instrument movements visible throughout

key_improvements: Needle loading: 30s → 10s (67% improvement — skill fully acquired)
Drain closure: maintained complete closure with the fastest time
All phases at personal bests simultaneously for the first time
Recovery from regression (postV6: 196s → PostV7: 142s) confirms the dip was fatigue, not skill loss
